name: MediaData
short_description: Media metadata management system using torrent files as the primary organizational structure for immutable library archiving.
long_description: MediaData is a comprehensive media metadata management system that uses torrent files as the foundational organizational structure. It creates immutable libraries with hash-based directories while generating Kodi/Jellyfin-compatible NFO metadata files. The system uses a unified folder approach where users provide directories to scan, and torrents are automatically identified by their .torrent extension and matched to media files. It supports multiple media types (movies, TV series, music, audiobooks, books, etc.) and integrates with external metadata sources like TMDB for automatic metadata fetching. The architecture emphasizes immutability through hash-based directory structures, hierarchical metadata identification from multiple sources, professional filename parsing using guessit, and comprehensive audit trails with JSON logging.

modules.description: Package initialization exporting public API including scanning, organization, metadata processing, and main MediaData interface.
Main orchestrator class providing high-level interface for complete media processing workflows including scanning, organization, and metadata fetching.
Command-line interface providing access to all MediaData functionality with support for process, scan, organize, metadata, status, and info commands.
Torrent scanning module integrating with torrent-scanner backend for persistent database storage and improved torrent matching capabilities.
Library organization functionality for moving torrents and data into hash-based directory structure with collision handling and dry-run support.
Metadata identification and import system with support for multiple external sources (TMDB, Goodreads, OpenLibrary) and hierarchical identification from NFO files and filenames.
Core utilities for torrent file processing including bencode decoding, info hash computation, NFO file parsing/writing, and filesystem management.
Default configuration settings including media extensions, metadata source priorities, and default paths for archive and temporary directories.
